sentences.text: Yeah, my uncle was in England.
And I took it personal.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After the war, he came to Hamburg once.
And since he was under my house and I didn't think it was the right idea to cut him down to size, but I told him I would bring him a few blocks on his way, and I let him then have it.
And also about my mother's sister.
And I told him that I really, if possible, never want to see him again.
I only saw him twice.
Well, I had to go to gardening school.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] It's difficult to explain.
It was a gardening school, which was attached to a cemetery.
And we also had to help with the cemetery.
So one got very proficient to dig graves or just, you know, dig smaller graves for urns and learn about agriculture.
And that went on for two years.
And after two years-- I was not alone.
There were about 15 other kids or whatever in my age, boys and girls.
Then it was over.
And then I had to go to something like a dressmaking school, where all the Jewish girls were and everybody learned how to sew.
Everything was in Hamburg, yeah.
There were other girls and we had a good a time as it was possible.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We did our work, I don't know, from 8 o'clock to 4:00, or from 9 o'clock to 5:00.
I don't know.
And then, in the afternoon, we had to do things, which we wanted to do and could do.
We were not allowed to go to a theater, not to a movie, not to opera, not to concert, nothing.
That was law.
Jews were not allowed.
There were many parks you couldn't go through.
They had signs-- dogs, bicycles, and Jews not allowed.
Lovely, isn't it?
OK.
You know, in other words, you don't want me in your park, I don't want to go there.
That's all.
To dwell on it is just no good.
We got together and we danced sometimes and we told stories.
We [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] had little parties.
But it was already curfew at the time, so we had to be home by 8 o'clock or 9 o'clock.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We tried to make it as pleasant as possible, which was difficult but it could be done.
No public transportation.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No, that was during the war.
No public transportation and no place to go.
We couldn't even go ice skating.
We went on the lake.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know which year it was, we couldn't even shop in normal stores anymore.
To go marketing, special stores were opened for us.
We all got ration cards, but no ration cards for clothing or for shoes.
And we had to make do with what we had, which was very difficult when you are a growing person.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We just learned to take somebody else's dress and make it fit for you.
Acquaintances, yes.
Friends, no.
The school friend I had, I would go to her house and we had to wear, already, this big star.
Her father always said, when you come to the foot of the street, open the coat and people don't see the star, thinking that nobody knows then who I am.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But many people wouldn't have that.
And if you would see somebody in the street, they couldn't stop to talk to you.
They would endanger their lives and our lives.
You couldn't even say hello and then, you know, start walking on.
That wasn't possible.
No, it must have been '39 sometime.
No, I only know that it had to be sewn on.
You couldn't just do it with-- with pins or so.
I'm sorry, I didn't bring it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A normal Jewish star in yellow, and in it was written, J-U-D-E, Jude.
And it had to be attached on the left side of anything what you have to wear.
Of course, yeah.
Yeah, that was when everything got rationed, like a week or so later.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is when we got our food stamps, or whatever you call it, our ration cards.
I think we got the same amount of food than the others, maybe a little bit less.
But as I said, nothing for clothing and nothing for this and nothing for that, only the food.
Normal food.
So and so much meat, so and so much potatoes, so and so much vegetables, milk, and that was all.
Yeah.
No.
No, they were run by an arm of another big chain, but they were known as the Jew Stores.
The quality was not-- I don't know whether quality was less or not, but it happened quite often that they were sold out.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so you didn't have enough meat, and the rations of meat were very small to begin with.
What do you mean?
Hamburg was not a very narcissistic city, so people were not too jubilant.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I don't know how it was in other cities.
Hamburg had a touch of communism in them.
And since it's a big harbor, their horizon is completely different as those who were inland or never saw other people.
So I believe it was not as hard for the people in Hamburg, in comparison as to those in Berlin and Wiesbaden, Baden-Baden, and all these other little cities.
I think they had still some trades, the Germans, with Holland, and with Sweden and Denmark.
But, of course, the overseas and stopped completely.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] That's all what I know.
Because many ships didn't come anymore.
They were forbidden, well, allowed or forbidden or whatever, because the Germans didn't have the money to pay for the merchandise.
So they wanted it but they didn't have it.
Personally, I hated it.
I hated the idea that it was so dangerous.
I hated the idea that everything was a risk until my mother explained to me is everybody and everyday is a risk in life.
This is just another risk.
She's very down to earth.
And when they had the bombardments in Hamburg, we all had to go into the basement for safety.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not allowed to go to their basement, so they had to find an extra little basement for myself and the maid we had.
My mother was in the hospital at the time.
And as it turned out, my little cellar was much more secure as the other one.
And then they realized what a ridiculous thing it was, so they wanted me to come back again.
And I said, no, no, it's more secure here.
I stay here.
You want to come here, come here.
Well, they started it in '39 sometime or '40.
I think the English.
Some intestinal thing.
There was a Jewish hospital.
Yeah.
It was a clinic of [NON-ENGLISH] before.
It was then designated with a Jewish hospital.
Also Jewish, yeah, doctors and nurses.
What else would you?
There was something like a community house.
It used to be a theater.
This is where Jews could meet and have little performances, but that's all.
I think one was, a smaller one, would not hold all the people there.

In '41, because in '41-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] yeah, I forgot to tell you that the Gestapo would always come to people's house, unannounced, of course, and look through everything for whether you had money or jewelry or whatever.
They felt free to do it.
Most of the time, they destroyed many things in these people's houses, treated these people terribly, hit them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The top man from the Gestapo, his name was Gertze.
And he was just-- he hated Jews.
And when he could, he just whacked them.
Oh, yes, quite a few times.
They would come in, two or three people.
We want to check the house.
And they would open every closet and every drawer and whatever it is, and it looks later on as if somebody came and wants to steal something.
And, usually, it was just to go, and that's it.
Except one time they came and-- there was like a [NON-ENGLISH], like a-- with drawers.
That was a thing my great-grandmother owned and my mother inherited it.
And they wanted to open it.
And my mother said, you just go ahead.
We cannot open it.
And unfortunately, they found a lot of silver in there.
And it was very bad.
So they took my mother.
And my mother surely thought she was done in for.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gertze said, you come with me.
And he was very intolerant to her.
And then, he took her someplace, and they took the silver and dropped it into the river.
Took my mother to the Gestapo and said nothing was found there and she came on home.
Why that was, I have no idea.
But whoever told them that there may be silver in this particular chest of drawers, I have no idea.
My mother was lucky.
I would say once a month.
Hello, and hello, and how are you?
We have to check again and they checked.
And go ahead.
Not that you would see it.
You wouldn't even ask for it.
They just came with a little thing, you know?
You know them already.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And as long as you treated them with, oh, you're back again, go make yourself comfortable.
They were so taken back, because most Jews were terribly afraid of them, but my mother wasn't and I wasn't, the country.
So they really didn't like to come to my house or my mother's house because they know they got kind of like the cold shoulder.
At one time they wanted to come in and I wouldn't let them in.
I said, do you have a search warrant or come with the police or something.
Because, you know, who the hell do you think you are?
That was my attitude then.
I guess I still have the same attitude in most of things.
How did they react when you said that?
Puzzled.
They were not used to being treated like half servants.
Oh, yes, they would.
He was always performer.
They were.
Probably they're, we did it.
It was all right.
And that brat was back in the house again, meaning me.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I then learned that you should never, never show when you're afraid.
Like dogs smell it when you're afraid, people feel it when you're afraid, and then they know they are the top dog or they can do whatever they want.
We had to leave the apartment because they wanted that all the Jewish people would live in designated houses, which were not together in one complex, but all around the city.
So Mother and I lived in one room with one kitchen with all the other people on the floor.
There was one bathroom.
It was a trying affair, but you make do, as simple as that.
How far from where you used to live was this new place?
Maybe-- when you drive it, it's less than seven minutes.
When you walk it, it's good over half hour, hour.
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] An officer of the SS wanted the apartment and people came to look at the apartment.
And since it was a duplex, he wanted it very badly.
And he even specified what to keep in the apartment, which could not be taken out.
And we had to leave until a certain day.
The end of-- no, it must have been the end of '41.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, '40, '41.
So, I mean, he specified he wanted all these chairs and all this stuff there.
And that was that.
We had to leave it there, no argument.
You mean in this--
In the new place.
I think four or five.
Yeah, I knew them.
Mother didn't know them.
I knew them.
They were-- I do know there were two brothers, each of them married.
One of them had angina pectoris, and the other one didn't.
And I know they were enemies.
Yeah.
Oh, it was terrible.
I think for the last 20 years.
So that I remember about them, but I don't know their name.
Until I got to Theresienstadt, which was in '42.
No, no, no.
No, no.
I had to work in the ham factory by then.
And I had to do the shift work, you know, three shifts.
And I was allowed to use the subway or I could use my bicycle.
And since I was incorrigibly naughty, and did all kinds of things, they thought it's justified to give me two people from the Gestapo to watch over me.
In other words, to escort me from my house to the factory and back.
Oh, yeah, I was paid.
But these people from the-- people who owned the factory would not allow the Gestapo to come in.
They had to stay exactly outside of the door.
Yeah, either that or they'd send some other, two others.
I must have kept four of them very busy.
Also, because I never do-- I would like to take the bicycle or the subway.
I would take the bicycle, they had to have it there, because I was always running off.
I had designated streets that I was allowed to go.
Who could bother with that?
So I really, you know, I was bad, enjoying every minute of it.
Were there ever any consequences?
Oh, yes, of course.
One day, I decided I wanted to have an ice cream.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I asked my escorts whether they would get me an ice cream.
And they said, no.
I said, would you please get me one.
And they're-- why don't you get it yourself?
That was a very wrong thing to say because there was a small restaurant by the lake, and I went in there and I said, I would like to have an ice cream.
And they said, oh, how nice to see you again.
Your friends are in the big restaurant across the lake.
And I said, oh.
There's a boat going.
Would you like to go?
Not sooner said but done.
So I went.

And then the consequences were that I was lost and they didn't know where I was and they questioned these people and they had to send somebody else to get me out of this other restaurant, where an awful lot of my friends were.
Then I was called to the Gestapo headquarters and I got quite a calling down.
Not to ever do it again or else.
What's the else?
You know?
And he just told me that-- Mr.
Gertze, that I was not to do it again.
I should promise him.
I promised him not to go into that little restaurant ever again.
I know I should not go to any restaurant.
Can I go home now?
Yeah, they took me out of some friends' houses.
But this was not a public place and they all said, tsk, tsk, tsk, tsk, now come on home.
It's soon curfew.
Because they were, again, had to give account of what I did.
Yeah, so I tried what I could just to have fun with these people, which amused me because I figured how dare these people to do that to me.
Well, one shift was from 10:00, no, from 6:00 to afternoon, I don't know, 2:00 or 3:00.
And the other one was from 2:00 or 3:00 to 11:00 or 12:00, and then was the night shift.
Oh, either of them.
I rotated, you know?
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember there was in air raids, which was eerie because I was not allowed to go to a public-- what do you call it-- bunker.
So I had to run wherever I wanted to go and I was stopped.
You have to go to a basement and I said, I will ride home, I'll ride home with my little star.
I just, you know, kind of hid it.
That, I remember.
OK.
